definition: The knowledge graph is a deterministic, cache-backed graph of the magus domain: projects, targets, spells, ops, charms, modules, methods, and diagnostics, connected by verified relations (depends_on, contains, uses, references, documents). Every node and edge is extracted or rubric-inferred from workspace sources - no LLM pass - so it is safe to rebuild implicitly and query instead of grepping.

nodes.label: ci
nodes.doc: 'ci' is the conventional anchor that `magus affected ci` keys off. magus does not hardcode its steps — compose them here with magus.needs. magus runs ci read-only. Each stage declares its own prerequisites so the DAG is expressed as edges: ci fans out lint/build/test in parallel; they each wait for format, which waits for generate, which waits for preflight. Shared deps run once.

nodes.label: sheetContains
nodes.doc: sheetContains reports whether an AppKit-coordinate point lands inside the panel — the WM uses it so a click outside dismisses the sheet.

nodes.label: sheetShow
nodes.doc: sheetShow renders the current keymap centered at (cx, cy) — AppKit coordinates of the active display's center.

nodes.label: applyConfig
nodes.doc: applyConfig copies a user Config's scalar settings into the live Runtime, resolving the cheat chord to flags/keycode and the enums to their string forms. Keybindings/execs/rules are left untouched — bind()/exec()/rule() manage those.

nodes.label: binPackWorkspace
nodes.doc: binPackWorkspace returns the first workspace in [lo, hi] (1-based, inclusive) whose managed-window count is below cap, or 0 if every one in range is full. `counts` is the per-workspace tally (0-based: counts[i] is workspace i+1, as wsCounts() returns). A rules hook uses it to spread otherwise-unplaced windows across workspaces — bin-packing them cap-per-workspace — instead of piling them onto one. Pure and order-independent, so it's testable without the live WM.

nodes.label: bindAt
nodes.doc: bindAt re-types an entry of Config.binds ([any]) at the read side.

nodes.label: chordName
nodes.doc: chordName renders a bind's chord back into config spelling ("cmd+shift+h").

nodes.label: defaultBuzzConfig
nodes.doc: defaultBuzzConfig is the starter config.buzz written for a fresh install (and by the tray's "Edit config"). It is intentionally brace-free — Buzz string literals treat `{` as interpolation, so a full Config{…} template can't live in a string; that lives in config.example.buzz instead. An empty config already runs the built-in defaults, so this just orients the user.

nodes.label: defaultConfigText
nodes.doc: The built-in defaults are expressed in the config language itself, so they double as living documentation of the syntax and ship the default keymap in code (there is no user text-config file). The chords track i3's stock config where macOS lets them: vim-key focus/move, $mod+N workspaces, $mod+shift+q kill, $mod+shift+e exit.

nodes.label: escapeBuzz
nodes.doc: escapeBuzz escapes s for inclusion in a Buzz double-quoted string literal: backslashes, quotes, and `{` (which starts interpolation). `}` is literal in a string on both gopherbuzz and upstream buzz — and upstream rejects `\}` as an escape — so it is deliberately left alone.

nodes.label: exec
nodes.doc: --- typed command constructors ---  These build the command-language string for bind() / rule() / onWindowRule(), so a config calls functions (typed, discoverable) instead of hand-writing commands. Any command can still be passed as a plain string if it isn't covered here.

nodes.label: keyName
nodes.doc: keyName reverses the keycode table for display ("h", "return", …).

nodes.label: loadConfig
nodes.doc: loadConfig reads the file at path; a missing or unreadable file falls back to the built-in defaults (i3 ships a default config too).

nodes.label: parseChord
nodes.doc: parseChord turns "cmd+shift+h" into [flags, keycode], or null when a modifier or key name is unknown.

nodes.label: parseRule
nodes.doc: parseRule parses `app=X title="Some Name" <command>`: any mix of the two criteria (bare or double-quoted values), then the command. Null when a criterion or the command is missing.

nodes.label: promptTarget
nodes.doc: promptTarget decides the bin-pack prompt: given the live per-workspace tally `counts` (0-based: index i = workspace i+1), the current workspace `curWs` (0-based), and the crowding `cap`, it returns the 1-based workspace to offer moving a new window to — or 0 for "don't prompt" (feature off, current workspace still under cap, or nowhere less crowded to go).

nodes.label: readConfigText
nodes.doc: readConfigText returns the config file's content, or null when it is missing or unreadable. Host errors like File.open unwind to catch the same way thrown Buzz errors do.

nodes.label: ruleMatches
nodes.doc: ruleMatches: empty criteria match anything; /regex/ criteria match their compiled pattern; everything else is a case-insensitive substring test.

nodes.label: rulesSource
nodes.doc: rulesSource renders app→command pairs as a window-rules hook — real Buzz source, the same thing a user would write by hand. `layout export` feeds it the live arrangement, closing the loop: arrange windows, export, and the placement comes back as editable rules instead of bespoke values.

nodes.label: splitCommands
nodes.doc: splitCommands splits a "cmd; cmd; cmd" sequence into individual trimmed commands, dropping empties.

nodes.label: takeValue
nodes.doc: takeValue reads a bare, double-quoted, or /slash-delimited/ value (the regex form may contain spaces); returns [value, remainder].

nodes.label: tokenize
nodes.doc: tokenize splits a config line into words, dropping whole-line comments and substituting `$name` variables. Substitution is longest-prefix per token so "$mod+h" resolves even though the token isn't exactly "$mod".

nodes.label: extras
nodes.doc: Settings can branch on ordinary Buzz logic; a later wm\configure() overrides the earlier one field-by-field. Wrap the branch in a function and call it: a bare top-level `if` is a gopherbuzz convenience that upstream Buzz does not accept, so keeping conditionals inside a function keeps the config portable across both.

nodes.label: showToast
nodes.doc: showToast pops a brief notification centered near the top of the tiling area and records when it should self-dismiss; the pump loop hides it once that time passes.

nodes.label: applyConfigArtifact
nodes.doc: applyConfigArtifact reads the ops the config recorded and replays them onto the live config (cfgEdit() -- the reload candidate during a reload, the live config at startup), in order. Scalars from a configure() are assigned directly (already resolved on the config side); binds/rules go through the same system setters the rest of the WM uses, so parsing and validation live in one place.

nodes.label: boxDouble
nodes.doc: boxDouble coerces a decoded JSON number to a double. jsonEncode stringifies an integral double (3.0) without a decimal point, so jsonDecode reads it back as an int; floatValue() would then miss it and return 0.0. Reading the int form and widening it recovers the value (a genuine fraction like 8.5 keeps its decimal and decodes as a double already).

nodes.label: cmdReload
nodes.doc: cmdReload is the only way configuration changes apply (i3-style: edit, then $mod+r — nothing watches files behind your back). It validates before committing: the text config parses into a *candidate*, config.buzz runs with its setters redirected at that candidate, and only if everything succeeded does the candidate become live — a broken edit keeps the WM on the previous configuration. On success the validated text config is snapshotted to config.last-good, so reverting a bad edit is one copy.

nodes.label: configArtifactPath
nodes.doc: configArtifactPath is where the user's config (running in its own process) leaves the ops it recorded, for this side to replay. Must match wm\artifactPath; the config process inherits this process's env (HOME/XDG), so configDir resolves to the same path on both sides.

nodes.label: handlePromptKey
nodes.doc: handlePromptKey answers the bar prompt. The window has been held offscreen and out of the tree since the prompt went up, so BOTH answers place it now: Return moves it to the offered workspace and switches there (one motion); anything else (esc, the TTL backstop) tiles it onto the current workspace after all. Either way the prompt comes down and the keyboard ungrabs on the next tick.

nodes.label: pauseReconcileFor
nodes.doc: pauseReconcileFor pauses auto-reconcile and arms a timer to resume it after `minutes`. Driven by the tray menu's Pause-reconciliation submenu; the poll loop re-enables once os\time passes state\wm().reconcileResumeAt.

nodes.label: pushChords
nodes.doc: pushChords publishes the swallow set to the event-tap helper: the active mode's bound chords plus the cheat chord, so a matched hotkey is consumed in the tap callback (the focused app never sees it). Called on startup, reload, and mode change.

nodes.label: restoreAllWindows
nodes.doc: restoreAllWindows un-minimizes every managed window and pulls any corner-parked one back onto the active display — so quitting never strands a window where it can't be reached. (A force-kill skips this; relaunch + `sweep` recovers them.)

nodes.label: resumeReconcile
nodes.doc: resumeReconcile clears a pause (timed or indefinite) and catches the layout up.

nodes.label: runBuzzConfig
nodes.doc: runBuzzConfig runs the user's config.buzz to configure this WM session. Called at startup and by cmdReload.  The config runs in its own isolated scope (in-process io\runFile by default), so it can't mutate our state directly; instead it records its settings to a JSON artifact via the wm package and we replay that artifact here. The artifact is cleared first so a failed config can't leave a previous run's ops applied; the replay only runs when the config ran cleanly.  Two runtimes, one code path, picked by c

nodes.label: runConfigInProcess
nodes.doc: runConfigInProcess runs the config with io\runFile, in this process. Self-contained (no external interpreter), so it works in a standalone/magus-compiled binary with no `buzz` CLI around. This is the default. Safe on gopherbuzz (incl. magus binaries); see runBuzzConfig's note on the upstream-binary caveat. Returns true if it ran.

nodes.label: runConfigSubprocess
nodes.doc: runConfigSubprocess runs the config as a separate `buzz` process (BUZZ), giving it a clean heap. Opt-in via the BUZZ env var, for runtimes where in-process io\runFile is unsafe (the upstream buzz binary). The config imports only pure-Buzz modules and emits interpreter-agnostic JSON, so any working buzz will do. BUBBLEGUM_LIB is the config's -L search path (where config.buzz/wm.buzz live; default the cwd). Returns true if the config process exited cleanly.

nodes.label: toggleReconcile
nodes.doc: toggleReconcile pauses/resumes the WM's automatic window management. Paused, it stops adopting new windows and retiling on events/timer (manual commands still work); resuming reconciles immediately. Driven by the `reconcile` command and the status-bar toggle.

nodes.label: writeLastGood
nodes.doc: writeLastGood snapshots the just-validated config.buzz next to itself.

nodes.label: cmdEval
nodes.doc: cmdEval runs a line of Buzz inside this very session: the script shares the WM's globals (the registry, the tree, every imported module), so it can inspect or rewire the live window manager. The interpreter is the feature — no other WM ships its own language in-process.

nodes.label: evalEcho
nodes.doc: evalEcho receives the wrapped expression's value; eval scripts call it implicitly. The result lands on the bar and in the log.

nodes.label: cmdFloating
nodes.doc: cmdFloating moves the focused window out of (or back into) the tree. `mode` is "toggle" (default), "on" (float -- idempotent), or "off" (tile -- idempotent). Idempotent modes let a rule float a window without un-floating it when the rule re-runs (rules apply / live reload). Floating windows keep whatever frame the user gives them; the scanner tracks them but the tiler never touches them.

nodes.label: cmdFrame
nodes.doc: cmdFrame floats the focused window at an exact position: `frame x y w h`. This is how exported rules carry coordinates -- a floating window's frame round-trips through `layout export` as a frame command.

nodes.label: cmdLayoutRestore
nodes.doc: cmdLayoutRestore reshapes the current workspace from the snapshot: saved leaves claim live windows by app name, unmatched leaves are pruned, and windows the snapshot doesn't know are appended.

nodes.label: cmdLayoutSave
nodes.doc: cmdLayoutSave snapshots the ACTIVE screen's workspaces (multi-display snapshots would need per-screen files; one screen keeps it predictable).

nodes.label: cmdMark
nodes.doc: cmdMark / cmdGoto: vim registers for windows. `mark a` tags the focused window; `goto a` jumps to it -- switching workspace and screen if needed.

nodes.label: cmdMoveScratchpad
nodes.doc: cmdMoveScratchpad sends the focused window to the scratchpad: parked like an inactive workspace, but belonging to none -- it comes back floating.

nodes.label: cmdRecallMinimized
nodes.doc: cmdRecallMinimized opens the recall picker over just the minimized windows (the ones the user sent to the Dock), each "#id app - title". Picking one runs recall: un-minimize, re-home onto the current workspace, and tile it back. The bar's indicator clicks into this.

nodes.label: cmdScratchpadShow
nodes.doc: cmdScratchpadShow toggles: pop the next scratchpad window centered over the current workspace, or stash the one already showing back.

nodes.label: cmdSnap
nodes.doc: cmdSnap floats the focused window into a Rectangle-style position within the tiled display: halves, quarters, thirds, two-thirds, max -- plus `snap center`, which keeps the window's size. The default binds mirror Rectangle.app's (ctrl+alt+...), so it can be uninstalled without retraining.

nodes.label: cmdSummon
nodes.doc: cmdSummon opens launcher over every managed window ("#id app -- title"), tagging the stashed ones (minimized, scratchpad, wsN on another workspace) so they read apart from the visible ones -- and so typing "min" filters to exactly what you lost. Picking one recalls it: the rofi window switcher, doubling as the un-minimize mechanism.

nodes.label: cmdSweep
nodes.doc: cmdSweep is the panic button ($mod+shift+s): bring every window that belongs on this workspace back into reach. It un-minimizes the ones stashed out of sight (cmd+M, the yellow button, an app minimizing itself) and pulls any window that drifted off the tiled display back on. The rescan re-adopts what the un-minimize surfaces; retile re-slots it. Other workspaces and the scratchpad are left alone: this recovers the *current* workspace, it doesn't collapse them all into one. The surgical counterpart is $mod+o

nodes.label: recall
nodes.doc: recall brings a window to the here-and-now -- the one door back for anything stashed out of sight: a window the user minimized (cmd+M, the yellow button, the Dock), one parked on an inactive workspace, or a floating scratchpad window. It un-minimizes, re-homes the window onto the current workspace, and tiles or centers it. The window palette ($mod+o) opens onto this, so no window can end up somewhere the WM -- and the user -- can't reach it.

nodes.label: restoreSession
nodes.doc: restoreSession runs once at startup, right after the initial adopt has tiled every window onto the current workspace. It pulls the windows the saved session placed elsewhere back off to their workspaces (and hides them), leaving the rest here. Window rules run first (during rescan), so a rule that already moved a window off the current workspace wins -- restoreSession only redistributes what's still here.

nodes.label: stampTags
nodes.doc: stampTags labels every leaf with its app name so the snapshot can be matched against live windows after a restart.

nodes.label: cmdFocusCycle
nodes.doc: cmdFocusCycle steps through the tree's leaves in order -- the alt-tab of a keyboard-only workflow, and the fallback when directional focus has no candidate (a lone column, say).

nodes.label: cmdResize
nodes.doc: cmdResize: `resize grow|shrink` adjusts the innermost split; with an axis (`resize grow width|height`, the arrow-key binds) it adjusts the nearest split along that axis instead, like i3.

nodes.label: crossScreenNeighbor
nodes.doc: crossScreenNeighbor scans other screens' leaves for the nearest window in the cone from the focused window's center; returns screen*1e6+id or -1.

nodes.label: syncFocus
nodes.doc: syncFocus follows clicks: whatever macOS says is focused becomes our focused leaf, so directional commands start from where the user actually is.

nodes.label: consoleLines
nodes.doc: consoleLines is Retool's "explore what's available": every command the keymap, rules, config.buzz, and the buzz> prompt can use — served by the live WM itself, so it can't go stale the way external docs do.

nodes.label: inspectLines
nodes.doc: inspectLines is what the inspector overlay renders: the live objects — screens, trees (tagged with app names), the registry, the effective config. Everything here is also reachable from eval; the overlay is the read-only window, the buzz> prompt is the write end.

nodes.label: toggleCheatsheet
nodes.doc: toggleCheatsheet shows or hides the keymap reference as a sticky overlay — the click-to-open counterpart to the hold-to-peek cheat chord (handleKey). Opened this way it stays up until the info icon is clicked again.

nodes.label: cmdInit
nodes.doc: cmdInit writes a starter config.buzz so a fresh install has a file to edit instead of running on the built-in defaults. Invoked as `bubblegum init`; always exits.

nodes.label: mkdirP
nodes.doc: mkdirP creates path and any missing parents. fs\makeDirectory makes one level at a time and throws on both a missing parent and an existing dir, so we walk the segments and swallow each step's error - a real failure (e.g. permission) resurfaces when the caller goes to write the file.

nodes.label: openConfigInEditor
nodes.doc: openConfigInEditor hands config.buzz to the default editor (the tray menu's "Edit config"). Writes the starter config first if the user is still on built-in defaults, so the editor never opens on a missing file.

nodes.label: writeDefaultConfig
nodes.doc: writeDefaultConfig writes the starter config.buzz to buzzConfigPath() if none exists, creating the directory. Returns true if a config is present afterward (already there or freshly written), false only on a write error. Shared by the `init` subcommand and the tray menu's "Edit config".

nodes.label: frameLabel
nodes.doc: frameLabel renders a window's live frame as `1200x800 @(0,26)` for log lines.

nodes.label: idForRef
nodes.doc: idForRef matches an AX handle against the registry. AX hands out a fresh ref for the same window on every scan, so identity is CFEqual, not address.

nodes.label: logAct
nodes.doc: logAct emits one stdout line per action the WM takes - commands, window events, adopt/evict, workspace switches, retiles. Idle reconciles that change nothing stay quiet. Same `bubblegum:` prefix as the status messages.

nodes.label: takeMatch
nodes.doc: takeMatch claims the first unclaimed live window whose app name equals the saved tag (case-insensitive); 0 when none is left.

nodes.label: winLabel
nodes.doc: winLabel renders a managed window for a log line: `#5 Finder "Downloads"`. Drops the title when empty/unreadable, and falls back to the bare id when the window is already gone (evict can race a closed window).

nodes.label: wsCounts
nodes.doc: wsCounts tallies managed windows per workspace -- shared by the bar render and the bar-click workspace mapping.

nodes.label: applyRuleCmd
nodes.doc: applyRuleCmd dispatches a typed rule command against the given window, temporarily pointing state\wm().focusedId at it (mirrors runCommandFor's contract).

nodes.label: applyRules
nodes.doc: applyRules runs the declarative `rule` lines, then the rules.buzz hook, against a freshly adopted window -- i3's for_window, programmable.

nodes.label: cmdLayoutExport
nodes.doc: cmdLayoutExport closes the loop: the live arrangement comes back as window rules -- runnable, editable Buzz (rules.generated.buzz) -- instead of bespoke values in a data file. Arrange by hand, export, done: every app you placed gets pinned to its workspace (floating windows export as floating) the next time it opens. First window of each app wins.

nodes.label: loadRules
nodes.doc: loadRules (re)loads rules.generated.buzz (written by `layout export`) and rules.buzz, re-registering hooks from scratch: an absent file simply drops out, a broken one reports and the WM keeps going. Runs at startup and on the explicit `reload` command -- nothing watches files behind your back.

nodes.label: onWindowRule
nodes.doc: onWindowRule registers a window hook written in Buzz -- the programmable half of window rules. rules.buzz (loaded into this very session, so this function is in its scope) calls it with a closure:  onWindowRule(fun (app: str, title: str, id: int) > [any] { if (app == "Music") { return [floating()]; } return []; });  Return a list of typed command values (see helpers above); return [] for no action. Hooks accumulate in registration order, so `layout export`'s generated rules and hand-written ones coexist (ha

nodes.label: reTest
nodes.doc: reTest compiles pat and tests it against text in one call -- false on a bad pattern instead of crashing. Use in place of reCompile(pat)!/reMatch pairs.

nodes.label: runCommandFor
nodes.doc: runCommandFor runs a command with the given window as its target (commands act on state\wm().focusedId); the previous focus is restored unless the command moved it on purpose.

nodes.label: adoptOn
nodes.doc: adoptOn inserts a window into a screen's live tree -- through the globals for the active screen, directly into stored state otherwise.

nodes.label: detachCurrent
nodes.doc: detachCurrent removes a window from the active tree (the globals).

nodes.label: detachOn
nodes.doc: detachOn removes a window from a screen's live tree (current workspace).

nodes.label: detachParked
nodes.doc: detachParked is detachCurrent against an inactive workspace's saved state on the window's own screen.

nodes.label: firstEmptyWorkspace
nodes.doc: firstEmptyWorkspace returns the nearest workspace (0-based) holding no windows, other than the current one, or -1 if every other workspace is occupied.

nodes.label: ignoreWindow
nodes.doc: ignoreWindow un-manages a window: evicts it from the tree and removes it from the managed registry so the next retile never places it.

nodes.label: maybePromptCrowded
nodes.doc: maybePromptCrowded raises the bar prompt for a window adopted this scan that stayed on the current workspace while that workspace is over binPackCap. Only the first such window is offered (one question at a time); explicitly-placed windows have already been moved off curWs by applyRules, so they're skipped.

nodes.label: promptText
nodes.doc: promptText renders the active prompt's message with a live seconds countdown to its auto-dismiss (state\promptTtlMs), so the user can see how long they have to answer before it decides for them.

nodes.label: raiseRelief
nodes.doc: raiseRelief holds a window aside (out of the tree, parked offscreen) and puts the "move to a roomier workspace?" question on the bar. handlePromptKey resolves it: Return moves it to `target` and follows; anything else tiles it back here. Shared by the count-based crowd check and the fit-based stack check below.

nodes.label: sendToWorkspace
nodes.doc: sendToWorkspace sends a specific window to workspace `ws` and hides it there (per hideMethod), without changing focus or following it. The relief counterpart to cmdMoveToWs, which acts on the focused window for an explicit, deliberate move.

nodes.label: stackedVictim
nodes.doc: stackedVictim returns a current-workspace window the tiler stacked coincident with another (the no-overlap fallback when windows don't fit), preferring one that is not focused so relief moves a background window, not the one in use. Returns 0 when nothing is stacked. Monocle and the stacked layout make windows coincident on purpose, so callers skip this when fullscreenId or `stacked` is set.

nodes.label: started
nodes.doc: started reports whether the WM has finished its initial adopt. Exposed to rules hooks so a rule can bin-pack only at startup and defer interactive arrivals to the prompt, e.g.  if (ws > 0 and !started()) { return "move workspace {ws}"; }

nodes.label: initScreens
nodes.doc: initScreens (re)builds the screen set from the config. On a reload that changes the picks, everything is released back to macOS and re-adopted fresh -- predictable beats clever here.

nodes.label: pickedDisplays
nodes.doc: pickedDisplays resolves the config into 1-based display indexes: tile_displays wins, `all` means every active display, otherwise the legacy single tile_display.

nodes.label: refreshTiledBounds
nodes.doc: refreshTiledBounds re-reads each tiled display's bounds (displays move, resolutions change); an unplugged pick falls back to the main display.

nodes.label: saveActive
nodes.doc: saveActive/loadActive swap the live globals with a screen's stored state.

nodes.label: screenForRef
nodes.doc: screenForRef returns the tiled-screen index whose bounds hold the window's center, or -1 (an untiled display: the window stays native).

nodes.label: tiledAppKit
nodes.doc: tiledAppKit converts the tiled bounds to AppKit coordinates (origin at the main display's bottom-left, y up) for the bar and launcher panels.

nodes.label: treeOf
nodes.doc: treeOf reads a screen's live tree (the globals for the active screen).

nodes.label: maybeSaveSession
nodes.doc: maybeSaveSession rewrites the session file only when the (window -> workspace) mapping actually changed - the signature is plain struct reads, no AX, so it is cheap to call every reconcile. Driven from tick(), so the file survives a crash, not just a clean quit.

nodes.label: parkRestored
nodes.doc: parkRestored hides a window placed on an inactive workspace, the same way a workspace switch would (state\wm().cfg.hideMethod). The "hide" (app-hide) method is reconciled in one pass by the caller, since it keys on the app, not the window.

nodes.label: readSession
nodes.doc: readSession parses the session file into (ws, app) entries; empty when there is no file (a first run, or the user deleted it).

nodes.label: saveSession
nodes.doc: saveSession writes one "<ws>\t<app>" line per tiled window. Floating and scratchpad windows are skipped - they are ephemeral, not part of the layout.

nodes.label: cfgEdit
nodes.doc: cfgEdit is what the config.buzz setters mutate: the reload candidate while one is being validated, the live config otherwise (startup).

nodes.label: configure
nodes.doc: The config.buzz API. configure() applies a typed Config (all the settings); bind()/rule() append; clearBinds() starts the keymap from zero for purists; the command constructors (config\focusLeft(), config\workspace(2), ...) build a bind's command. The default keymap ships embedded (config\defaultConfigText), so a user config only sets what it wants to change.

nodes.label: ensureAccessibility
nodes.doc: ensureAccessibility checks the one permission bubblegum needs and walks the user through granting it: trigger macOS's native prompt (which registers bubblegum in the Settings list), deep-link System Settings to the exact pane, then poll the live trust state until the toggle flips. No SIP changes, no root — one checkbox.

nodes.label: applyFrame
nodes.doc: applyFrame skips the AX round-trip when the window is already in place, so the poll-driven retile is a cheap no-op for a settled layout.

nodes.label: floatOversized
nodes.doc: floatOversized is the honest-float half: a window whose learned minimum exceeds the whole tiling area can never fit the grid (a fixed-size dialog bigger than the usable space). Rather than let it overflow and fight the layout, drop it out of the tree and float it centered. Never floats the last tiled window.

nodes.label: learnWindowMins
nodes.doc: learnWindowMins reads each leaf window's settled frame and, if it clamped LARGER than the slot it was last given, records that as the window's learned minimum (monotonic). It then stamps the leaves so the next assign stacks exactly -- frame-readback is the only way macOS exposes a window's minimum size (there is no AXMinSize). Runs once per retile, before assign, reading the frame each window settled into since the previous retile.

nodes.label: pushUndo
nodes.doc: pushUndo snapshots the active screen's tree before a mutating command; `undo` pops back, pruning windows that closed in between.

nodes.label: retile
nodes.doc: retile lays out every tiled screen: the active one from the live globals, the rest from their stored state. Golden mode follows the global focus, so only the active screen reflows around it.

nodes.label: seedDims
nodes.doc: seedDims re-assigns the tiling rect to a tree mid-build so the NEXT split's chooseOrient sees real leaf shapes instead of zeros. Without it, a tree built in one pass before the first retile (cold start with many windows, undo, restore) splits every window horizontally -- full-height columns too narrow for apps to honor, so they overlap. assign here only sets geometry; no window moves until retile.

nodes.label: tilingRect
nodes.doc: tilingRect derives a screen's usable tiling rectangle from its CG frame `cg` ([x, y, w, h]) -- insetting the outer gap, the top padding (menu bar) and the status bar. Shared by retileOne and the build-time seedDims below.

nodes.label: updateRing
nodes.doc: updateRing keeps the focus border (focusring.buzz) glued to the focused window: the tree's assigned frame when tiled, the real frame when floating. Hidden while a fullscreen monocle owns the screen.

nodes.label: buildTrayMenu
nodes.doc: buildTrayMenu rebuilds state\wm().trayRows (what's drawn) and state\wm().trayRowActs (what each row does) for the current state: a status header, a display-summary info row, the reconcile control (Resume when paused, else Pause with its inline duration submenu), then Edit config.

nodes.label: displaySummary
nodes.doc: displaySummary returns a one-line description of which displays are being tiled, read from live state so it reflects config changes without a restart.

nodes.label: fmtClock
nodes.doc: fmtClock renders a millisecond span as M:SS (clamped at zero).

nodes.label: handleTrayClick
nodes.doc: handleTrayClick routes a left-click (global CG coords) for the menu-bar tray item: a click on the tray glyph opens the drop-down; with the menu open, a click picks a row or (anywhere else) dismisses it. Returns true if it consumed the click, so the caller skips the status-bar handler.

nodes.label: openTrayMenu
nodes.doc: openTrayMenu drops the menu just under the tray icon: its right edge aligned with the icon's, dropping downward. f is windows\viewFrameCG's [x,y,w,h, primaryH] for the tray button (CG top-left + main-display height in points). It records the menu's fixed top edge; showTrayMenu (re)draws and grows the panel downward as the row set changes (the inline submenu expands/collapses).

nodes.label: resumeRowText
nodes.doc: resumeRowText is the Resume menu item, carrying the live countdown to auto-resume when the pause is timed. Ticked in place by the pump loop so the menu does not rebuild every second.

nodes.label: showTrayMenu
nodes.doc: showTrayMenu rebuilds the row model for the current state and (re)draws the open menu, keeping its top edge fixed while the height tracks the row count.

nodes.label: trayHeaderText
nodes.doc: trayHeaderText is the Docker-style status line: running or paused. The pause countdown lives on the Resume row now (resumeRowText), not in this header.

nodes.label: adoptFloating
nodes.doc: adoptFloating promotes a dragged floating window into the grid: it lands next to the tile under the drop point (snapping in where you let go), or takes the default slot if the grid is empty or the drop missed every tile. The mouse counterpart to cmdFloating("off") / $mod+shift+space.

nodes.label: detectMaximize
nodes.doc: detectMaximize promotes a managed tiled window the user blew up to full size (the green-button zoom, a double-click on the title bar, a manual drag-resize out to the edges) into bubblegum's own monocle fullscreen, rather than fighting it on the next retile or mis-learning its minimum as screen-sized. Returns true if it entered fullscreen so the event path can retile; tick retiles regardless.

nodes.label: dragHold
nodes.doc: dragHold reports that the user is holding the left mouse button down (a window drag in progress). While it is true the reconcile freezes its layout work, so a window being dragged is never snapped back and its slot stays reserved: the other windows do not reflow to fill the gap until release. A safety timeout resumes the reconcile if a mouse-up is ever missed.

nodes.label: drainWindowEvents
nodes.doc: drainWindowEvents consumes the AX observer queue each pump and reacts at once, so windows tile the instant they appear instead of waiting for the reconcile timer. A burst (an app opening several windows) is coalesced into a single reconcile. A bare move no longer triggers a full reconcile (our own retile moves windows and would feed back), but it is checked for a manual maximize; ordinary drags are handled by the reflow path (handleRelease).

nodes.label: floatingAt
nodes.doc: floatingAt returns the floating current-workspace window under (x, y) — the one the user just dropped — or 0 if the drop didn't land on a floating window.

nodes.label: handleBarClick
nodes.doc: handleBarClick routes a left-click (global CG coords) that lands on the status bar: the reconcile toggle at the right edge, or a workspace cell on the left.

nodes.label: handleKey
nodes.doc: handleKey processes one key event dequeued from the helper (etype is the CGEventType: 10 key-down, 11 key-up, 12 flags-changed). Swallowing already happened in the tap callback (against the chord table pushChords() publishes, plus the launcher grab), so this only reacts.

nodes.label: handleMouse
nodes.doc: handleMouse hands focus to the hovered window — X11's focus-follows-mouse, which macOS still doesn't offer. The cursor position comes from the (listen-only) mouse tap via the helper; hit-testing is pure geometry against our own assigned frames, throttled so a fast move costs a couple of compares.

nodes.label: handleOverlayClick
nodes.doc: handleOverlayClick dismisses an open centered overlay (launcher, inspector, cheatsheet) when a click lands outside it — the macOS "click out to close" gesture. Returns true if an overlay was up so the click is consumed (it never also toggles the bar or tray underneath). The click coords are global CG (top-left origin); the overlays know their frames in AppKit (bottom-left), so convert with the main display's height, same as tiledAppKit.

nodes.label: handleRelease
nodes.doc: handleRelease implements drag-to-reflow on a left-mouse-up that ended a drag. First: a floating window shoved to a screen edge is promoted into the grid (drag-to-tile). Otherwise the dragged tiled window swaps with the window whose slot it was dropped on (i3-style move-by-mouse). Either way the drop snaps into the grid; nothing overlaps.

nodes.label: modsMatch
nodes.doc: modsMatch compares exactly the four modifier bits: extra device-state bits in CGEventFlags must not break matching, and a bind without shift must not fire when shift is held.

nodes.label: nearTilingEdge
nodes.doc: nearTilingEdge reports whether a drop point sits in that snap band around the active screen's tiling rect. Corners are just where two edges meet — they qualify.

nodes.label: tick
nodes.doc: tick is the periodic scan: re-arm the taps, re-read display bounds, follow the system focus, adopt/evict windows, retile, and refresh the chrome. Driven by the poll loop in main every state\wm().cfg.pollMs.

nodes.label: toggleOrientation
nodes.doc: toggleOrientation sets how the focused window's container will split NEXT — the i3 `split` toggle. It does NOT rearrange the windows already on screen; it only records, on the focused leaf, whether the next window opened here lands side by side or stacked below. The choice is consumed by splitLeaf when that next window arrives. Driven by clicking the orientation icon in the status bar; the `split h|v` command sets it explicitly.

nodes.label: cmdMoveToWs
nodes.doc: cmdMoveToWs sends the focused window to workspace n (0-based) and parks it there; the window leaves the active tree immediately.

nodes.label: cmdWorkspace
nodes.doc: cmdWorkspace switches to workspace n (0-based): save the active tree into its slot, park its windows, restore the target's windows and tree. Re-requesting the current workspace bounces to the previous one -- i3's workspace_auto_back_and_forth, always on.

nodes.label: parkLeaving
nodes.doc: parkLeaving hides the windows of the workspace being left (state\wm().curWs at call time), per state\wm().cfg.hideMethod. The "hide" (app-hide) method is reconciled in showArriving (after state\wm().curWs flips) since it keys on the destination, not the origin.

nodes.label: reconcileAppHide
nodes.doc: reconcileAppHide ("hide" method): every app with a managed window on the current workspace is shown; every app without one is hidden (cmd+H). A window of a shown app that lives on another workspace (the app spans workspaces) is corner-parked, since app-hide is all-or-nothing. We track what we hid (state\wm().hiddenPids) because AXHidden's read lags its async set.

nodes.label: showArriving
nodes.doc: showArriving reveals the windows of the workspace just switched to (n == state\wm().curWs).

nodes.label: gridCells
nodes.doc: gridCells lays n cells out in the smallest near-square grid that fits the rect: cols is the smallest c with c*c >= n, rows is ceil(n/cols). Pure geometry, no AX, so it is unit-tested directly. Returns one [x, y, w, h] per cell in row-major order.

nodes.label: layoutBackLayer
nodes.doc: layoutBackLayer positions every inactive-workspace tiled window into the background grid on its own screen. Idempotent: a window keeps its cell across calls (same membership -> same order -> same frame), so re-running it on every switch and reconcile tick doesn't visibly move a settled window. Floating windows are left to parkLeaving (it minimizes them, preserving their hand- placed frame); user-minimized windows stay in the Dock.

nodes.label: raiseActiveLayer
nodes.doc: raiseActiveLayer asserts the z-index: every window on the current workspace is raised above the background grid, the focused one raised last so it sits on top of its peers. This is the entropy fighter -- macOS reshuffles the global stack whenever the user cmd-tabs or a background app self-activates, and there is no AXLower to push the intruder down, so we can only re-raise what we want on top. Called after every retile and on the reconcile tick.

nodes.label: ringShow
nodes.doc: ringShow frames (x, y, w, h) — CG global coordinates of the window's SLOT — with a rounded border of the given stroke width, drawn inset (the stroke fills the band just inside the slot edge, where the tiler reserved space by shrinking the window). radius is the macOS window corner radius (from config); mainH converts to AppKit's bottom-left origin.

nodes.label: inspContains
nodes.doc: inspContains reports whether an AppKit-coordinate point lands inside the panel — the WM uses it so a click outside dismisses the inspector.

nodes.label: inspShow
nodes.doc: inspShow renders lines (the WM builds them from live state) centered at (cx, cy) in AppKit coordinates; call again while visible to refresh.

nodes.label: appNames
nodes.doc: appNames scans the standard application folders, deduplicates, strips the .app suffix, and sorts case-insensitively.

nodes.label: build
nodes.doc: build creates the bar once, centered near the top of the tiled display's area (ax/ay/aw/ah, AppKit coordinates: origin bottom-left, y up).

nodes.label: detached
nodes.doc: detached runs a shell command without blocking the event tap.

nodes.label: filterNames
nodes.doc: filterNames ranks matches by fuzzyScore plus a frecency boost: apps launched through launcher before sort ahead at equal match quality (the boost is small, so a clearly better letter-match still wins).

nodes.label: fuzzyScore
nodes.doc: fuzzyScore is the fzf-family ranking: a greedy in-order character match with positional bonuses, tuned for "type a few letters of the app you mean". -1 means no match; higher is better. Per character matched: +16 base, +24 extra at a word boundary (start, or after space/-/_/./(), so "vsc" loves "Visual Studio Code"), +12 extra when consecutive with the previous match (typing real prefixes wins), and each gap between matches costs -1 (tighter matches win). Case-insensitive throughout.

nodes.label: historyBoost
nodes.doc: historyBoost: +6 per prior launch, capped at +30 — enough to float your daily apps over alphabetical neighbors, never enough to beat a much better letter-match.

nodes.label: launcherContains
nodes.doc: launcherContains reports whether an AppKit-coordinate point lands inside the panel — the WM uses it to tell a dismissing outside-click from an inside one.

nodes.label: launcherDismiss
nodes.doc: launcherDismiss closes the bar from outside the keyboard path — the WM calls it when a click lands outside the panel (escape still closes it from launcherKey).

nodes.label: launcherEval
nodes.doc: launcherEval opens the bar as a live Buzz prompt: the line is handed to the WM's eval callback and runs inside the running interpreter session.

nodes.label: launcherKey
nodes.doc: launcherKey handles one key-down while the bar is visible. Every key is consumed (the bar owns the keyboard, dmenu-style); the return value tells the tap callback whether it was ours at all.

nodes.label: launcherOnEval
nodes.doc: launcherOnEval registers the function Eval-mode lines are handed to.

nodes.label: launcherWindows
nodes.doc: launcherWindows is rofi's window switcher: fuzzy-pick any managed window (rows provided by the WM, "#id app — title") and jump to it. The chosen row goes to the registered pick callback.

nodes.label: moveSel
nodes.doc: moveSel slides the selection pill behind the current row, or parks it offscreen when there are no matches (Run/Eval modes, or a query that filters to nothing).

nodes.label: shellQuote
nodes.doc: shellQuote single-quotes s for /bin/sh, the only quoting that disables every shell metacharacter; an embedded quote becomes '\''.

nodes.label: strLess
nodes.doc: strLess is lexicographic string ordering by code unit. Upstream buzz has no `<` operator on str, so the alphabetical tie-break is spelled out here; both runtimes provide str.byte.

nodes.label: typedKeyFor
nodes.doc: typedKeyFor maps a key-down's keycode to the character it types, or null for a non-typing key. Exported for launcher_test.buzz.

nodes.label: applyGolden
nodes.doc: applyGolden re-proportions the focused window's ancestor splits to the golden ratio — the focused side takes φ⁻¹ ≈ 0.618 — clamped so neither side of a split drops below the configured minimum size along its axis. Frames must be assigned before the call (the clamp reasons in pixels); assign again afterwards to apply the new ratios. True when target was found in the subtree.

nodes.label: assign
nodes.doc: assign walks the tree handing each node its frame. A container splits along its orientation UNLESS a child slot would fall below the larger of the global floor (minW × minH) and the child subtree's largest learned per-window minimum — then it STACKS (both children and their subtrees take the full rect, coincident). This is the no-overlap guarantee: the WM never produces a slot a window would overflow into its neighbour. The floor catches the gross case immediately; the learned mins (stamped by the WM fro

nodes.label: cloneTree
nodes.doc: cloneTree deep-copies a tree, ids included — the unit of layout undo (snapshots via encodeTree lose ids by design; undo must keep them).

nodes.label: collectLeaves
nodes.doc: collectLeaves appends every leaf to `out` (pass a `mut []`); the untyped list is the Buzz idiom for an accumulator, re-typed at the read side.

nodes.label: decodeTree
nodes.doc: decodeTree rebuilds a tree from a serialize\jsonDecode Boxed. Leaves come back with id 0 and their saved tag; the caller matches tags to live windows and prunes what it cannot match (pruneZeroLeaves).

nodes.label: encodeTree
nodes.doc: encodeTree renders the tree as nested maps; an empty tree is a marker map (JSON has no useful way to round-trip a bare null through the Boxed API).

nodes.label: grow
nodes.doc: grow widens the target window's share of its innermost split by delta (negative delta shrinks), clamped so a pane never collapses entirely.

nodes.label: growAxis
nodes.doc: growAxis widens target's share of the nearest enclosing split with the given orientation (i3's `resize grow width/height`): width follows horizontal splits, height vertical ones. The innermost matching ancestor wins; false when no ancestor splits along that axis.

nodes.label: insertAt
nodes.doc: insertAt splits the leaf with the target id into a container holding the old window and the new one. Returns false when the target isn't in this subtree, so callers can fall back (bubblegum retries at the last leaf).

nodes.label: lastLeaf
nodes.doc: lastLeaf is the insertion fallback: the most recently split position.

nodes.label: leafAt
nodes.doc: leafAt returns the id of the leaf whose assigned frame contains (x, y), or 0. Drives drag-to-reflow: the slot a window was dropped on. For a stacked group (coincident leaves) it returns the first member, which is a fine swap target.

nodes.label: maxMinW
nodes.doc: maxMinW/maxMinH return the largest learned minimum (Node.minW/minH) across a subtree's leaves — the width/height the widest/tallest window in that subtree needs. assign uses these so it never hands a window a slot below its real min.

nodes.label: neighbor
nodes.doc: neighbor returns the id of the nearest window whose center lies within the 90° cone in direction (dx, dy) from the focused window's center — i3's geometric `focus left/right/up/down`, not tree order. 0 when there is none.

nodes.label: pendingOrient
nodes.doc: pendingOrient reports how the target leaf would split next: its explicit `pick` if set, else along its longer axis (Horiz when wider than tall). Null when the leaf is absent. Drives the status-bar orientation indicator.

nodes.label: pruneMissing
nodes.doc: pruneMissing drops leaves whose id is not in keep (windows that closed since the snapshot), collapsing splits — undo against a changed world.

nodes.label: pruneZeroLeaves
nodes.doc: pruneZeroLeaves drops every leaf that stayed unmatched (id 0), collapsing single-child splits, and returns what survives (possibly null).

nodes.label: removeAt
nodes.doc: removeAt collapses the parent of the target leaf into the surviving sibling. A root that is itself the target leaf is the caller's case (the tree becomes empty, which only the root's owner can express).

nodes.label: snapFrame
nodes.doc: snapFrame maps a snap position name to a frame within the given bounds: halves, quarters, thirds, two-thirds, and max — the Rectangle.app catalog, so Rectangle can be uninstalled. Null for an unknown position.

nodes.label: stackAll
nodes.doc: stackAll lays every leaf in a subtree at the same rect — a stacked group where only the top (focused) window shows. Used when a container can't be split without producing an unusably small slot.

nodes.label: swapIds
nodes.doc: swapIds exchanges two windows' positions in the tree (i3 `move`): only the leaf ids move, the split geometry stays put.

nodes.label: treeLines
nodes.doc: treeLines renders the tree as indented text for the live inspector: splits show orientation and ratio, leaves show their tag (the app name, stamped by the caller) or id. Pure, so the inspector's heart is testable anywhere.

nodes.label: debug
nodes.doc: debug logs a milestone (shown at BUBBLEGUM_LOG=debug or trace).

nodes.label: enabled
nodes.doc: enabled reports whether any maintainer logging is on. Guard a log call with it when building the message is itself expensive (a hot-loop path, a frame read), so a quiet run pays nothing for the string it would have printed.

nodes.label: trace
nodes.doc: trace logs fine-grained detail (shown only at BUBBLEGUM_LOG=trace).

nodes.label: traceEnabled
nodes.doc: traceEnabled reports whether trace-level (the noisy tier) is on.

nodes.label: send
nodes.doc: send..sendPointSize forward to the per-shape objc_msgSend modules (see header).

nodes.label: uiBorderPanel
nodes.doc: uiBorderPanel creates a borderless, always-on-top window that draws ONLY a rounded-rectangle border — clear fill, colored stroke: the focus ring. radius is the corner radius (match the window corner radius); color the stroke color. The stroke width is set separately (uiSetBorderWidth) since it tracks the live config. uiSetPanelFrame moves/resizes it; the backing layer re-rounds at the new size.

nodes.label: uiColorAlpha
nodes.doc: uiColorAlpha returns a copy of color at the given alpha (NSColor colorWithAlphaComponent:), e.g. the accent color softened for a highlight pill.

nodes.label: uiFill
nodes.doc: uiFill adds a layer-backed rounded rectangle to the panel: a plain filled background (no text, no border), e.g. a subtle tint behind the workspace cells. Added before the labels so it sits beneath them.

nodes.label: uiHide
nodes.doc: uiHide orders the panel out and cycles the autorelease pool, releasing the NSStrings accumulated by uiSetText while the panel was up.

nodes.label: uiIgnoreMouse
nodes.doc: uiIgnoreMouse makes a panel click-through — overlay chrome (the focus ring, the cheat sheet) must never steal a click from the window below.

nodes.label: uiImageFromFile
nodes.doc: uiImageFromFile loads an NSImage from a path (absolute or CWD-relative) and marks it as a template so macOS auto-adapts it for light/dark menu bars. Returns null if the file does not exist or cannot be decoded.

nodes.label: uiImageView
nodes.doc: uiImageView adds an NSImageView holding image to the panel, tinted with color (template images render in the tint). Returns its handle so the image can change.

nodes.label: uiInit
nodes.doc: uiInit stands up the shared NSApplication as an accessory (no Dock icon, windows allowed) and opens an autorelease pool for the messages that follow.

nodes.label: uiLabel
nodes.doc: uiLabel adds a static text row to the panel: monospaced (dmenu heritage), no border, no background, given point size and text gray level.

nodes.label: uiMaterialPanel
nodes.doc: uiMaterialPanel creates a borderless, always-on-top panel whose backdrop is a translucent NSVisualEffectView of the given material (set as the content view), so anything added afterward lands inside it and picks up vibrancy. material is an NSVisualEffectMaterial raw value (5 = menu, 6 = popover, …).

nodes.label: uiMenuBarPanel
nodes.doc: uiMenuBarPanel is the status bar's backdrop on the macOS theme: an OPAQUE window painted with the system windowBackgroundColor (which adapts to light/dark). The top menu bar only ever frosts the wallpaper, so on a solid desktop it looks opaque; an opaque bottom bar matches that instead of frosting the windows behind it (which read as busy/translucent). i3 uses a solid dark uiPanel.

nodes.label: uiNamedColor
nodes.doc: uiNamedColor fetches a dynamic system color by its NSColor class-method name (labelColor, secondaryLabelColor, controlAccentColor, …). These adapt to the view's effective appearance, so text on the menu-material bar follows light/dark.

nodes.label: uiOverlayPanel
nodes.doc: uiOverlayPanel is the themed backdrop for floating overlays (cheatsheet, inspector, launcher, tray menu): a frosted popover material on macOS, a solid dark panel on i3.

nodes.label: uiPanel
nodes.doc: uiPanel creates a borderless, always-on-top panel of the given frame and background gray/alpha. Returns its handle.

nodes.label: uiSetBorderWidth
nodes.doc: uiSetBorderWidth sets the stroke width of a uiBorderPanel's rounded border (the border is drawn inside the layer bounds, so it stays within the panel frame).

nodes.label: uiSetCornerRadius
nodes.doc: uiSetCornerRadius updates a uiBorderPanel's corner radius. The stroke is drawn inside the bounds, so the border's INNER radius is (radius - borderWidth) — set radius to windowRadius + borderWidth for the inner edge to hug the window.

nodes.label: uiSetFillColor
nodes.doc: uiSetFillColor recolors a uiFill view in place — its color lives on the backing layer as a CGColor (not the NSView's backgroundColor), so it needs the same path uiFill used. Used for the tray menu's status dot (green running / amber paused).

nodes.label: uiSetFrame
nodes.doc: uiSetFrame repositions and resizes a view within its superview (AppKit coords).

nodes.label: uiSetPanelColor
nodes.doc: uiSetPanelColor sets a panel/view's background color (used to tint the focus ring's edge panels with the theme accent).

nodes.label: uiSetPanelFrame
nodes.doc: uiSetPanelFrame repositions and resizes a panel (AppKit coordinates).

nodes.label: uiSetSystemFont
nodes.doc: uiSetSystemFont swaps a label to the proportional system font (SF) at the given size — what macOS menus use, so bar/menu text matches the OS rather than the monospaced default uiLabel ships with.

nodes.label: uiSetSystemFontWeighted
nodes.doc: uiSetSystemFontWeighted is uiSetSystemFont with an explicit NSFontWeight (-1.0…1.0; 0.0 regular, 0.3 semibold, 0.4 bold) — for overlay titles that want a touch more weight than the body text around them.

nodes.label: uiSetTextColor
nodes.doc: uiSetTextColor overrides a label's text color, e.g. with a dynamic system color from uiNamedColor so it follows the appearance.

nodes.label: uiSetToolTip
nodes.doc: uiSetToolTip attaches a hover tooltip to a view. Best-effort on the bar: a borderless, non-activating panel may never receive the mouse-moved/tracking events AppKit needs to fire the tooltip, so this can be a no-op there.

nodes.label: uiStatusItem
nodes.doc: uiStatusItem puts bubblegum in the menu bar (top right): an NSStatusBar item with a text title — presence plus a live glyph of state. Returned retained (status items vanish if released). Click handling would need a runtime ObjC class for the target/action pair; the indicator stays passive instead.

nodes.label: uiStatusItemButton
nodes.doc: uiStatusItemButton returns the status item's NSStatusBarButton (an NSView) — the click target whose on-screen frame locates the menu-bar icon.

nodes.label: uiStatusItemSetImage
nodes.doc: uiStatusItemSetImage puts an image (e.g. an SF Symbol) on the menu-bar item's button. Status-bar buttons render template images monochrome and auto-adapt to light/dark, so no tint is needed.

nodes.label: uiSymbolImage
nodes.doc: uiSymbolImage loads an SF Symbol (system template image) by name at a point size and font weight, e.g. "magnifyingglass". Returns null if the symbol is unknown.

nodes.label: uiThemedLabel
nodes.doc: uiThemedLabel is uiLabel with theme-aware color: on i3 it keeps the given gray; on macOS it swaps to a dynamic system color (labelColor for bright grays ≥ 0.9, secondaryLabelColor for dimmer ones) so overlay text follows light/dark.

nodes.label: clearChords
nodes.doc: clearChords / addChord publish the swallow set: the helper returns NULL (swallows) for a key-down matching one of these chords, so a bound hotkey never reaches the focused app — i3's behavior, decided in the tap callback.

nodes.label: installTaps
nodes.doc: installTaps creates the key + mouse taps. Returns false when the key tap is refused — i.e. the Accessibility permission is missing.

nodes.label: pollClick
nodes.doc: pollClick reports a pending left-mouse-down (one per call); clickX/clickY read its location (global, top-left origin). Drives the clickable status bar.

nodes.label: pollKey
nodes.doc: pollKey dequeues one key event: returns its CGEventType (10 key-down, 11 key-up, 12 flags-changed), or 0 when the queue is empty. The keycode/flags of the dequeued event are then read with keycode()/flags().

nodes.label: pollMouse
nodes.doc: pollMouse reports whether the cursor moved since the last poll; mouseX/mouseY read the latest location (global, top-left origin).

nodes.label: pollRelease
nodes.doc: pollRelease reports a pending left-mouse-up (the drop of a drag); releaseX/Y read its location. Drives drag-to-reflow.

nodes.label: pollWindowEvent
nodes.doc: pollWindowEvent dequeues one window event: a winCreated/winDestroyed/… type, or 0 when the queue is empty. windowEventArg() then reads its pid-or-id.

nodes.label: pump
nodes.doc: pump runs the run loop for `seconds`: the taps deliver into the helper's queue and the AppKit overlays redraw. Call once per WM loop iteration.

nodes.label: reenableTaps
nodes.doc: reenableTaps re-arms either tap macOS disabled (slow callback, sleep, login).

nodes.label: setGrab
nodes.doc: setGrab toggles the launcher's keyboard grab: while on, every key-down is swallowed (the focused app sees nothing) and queued for the launcher.

nodes.label: watchApp
nodes.doc: watchApp registers AX observers for an app's window lifecycle (window created, focus changed). Idempotent per pid; call when a pid is first seen.

nodes.label: watchWindow
nodes.doc: watchWindow registers per-window observers (destroyed, moved, minimize) tagged with the managed id, so the drained event names the window directly. Call once when a window is first adopted.

nodes.label: cfNumber
nodes.doc: cfNumber reads a CFNumber through an out-parameter; -1 when ref is null.

nodes.label: cfToStr
nodes.doc: cfToStr copies a CFString into a Buzz str, byte by byte out of pinned memory (titles and subroles are short; 512 bytes of UTF-8 is plenty).

nodes.label: copyAttr
nodes.doc: copyAttr copies an AX attribute value; the returned ref is owned by the caller (CF "Copy" rule) and must be released. null when the attribute is unset.

nodes.label: dataSym
nodes.doc: dataSym resolves a process-global CF/AX data symbol by name and returns the CFTypeRef it stores. See the COMPAT note in the header.

nodes.label: dataSymAddr
nodes.doc: dataSymAddr returns a data symbol's own address (C's `&name`) rather than the pointer it stores — for struct-valued globals (the CFDictionary callbacks) whose address, not contents, is what the C API wants.

nodes.label: onMacOS
nodes.doc: onMacOS gates the FFI before any framework call, so an off-platform run is one friendly line instead of a dlopen error from deep inside a binding.

nodes.label: openLib
nodes.doc: openLib dlopen's a library by verbatim path (mode is an RTLD_* flag set). Exported because upstream buzz makes a zdef'd symbol callable only inside the module that declares it, yet forbids declaring it twice — so dlopen lives here once and other modules (e.g. cocoa loading AppKit) reach it through this.

nodes.label: openAccessibilitySettings
nodes.doc: openAccessibilitySettings deep-links System Settings to Privacy & Security → Accessibility — the exact pane with our toggle — via the public URL scheme.

nodes.label: promptAccessibility
nodes.doc: promptAccessibility asks macOS to show its native Accessibility permission dialog (which also registers this process in the Settings list, so the user only has to flip the toggle), and returns the current trust state.  The options dictionary is built exactly like the C call site would: { kAXTrustedCheckOptionPrompt: kCFBooleanTrue } with the CF-type callbacks.

nodes.label: appHidden
nodes.doc: appHidden reads whether an app is currently hidden (the AXHidden attribute is readable, not just settable) — so the WM can query hide state instead of tracking it blindly.

nodes.label: appName
nodes.doc: appName returns the owning application's name (its AX root element title), the stable identity window rules and layout snapshots match on.

nodes.label: appWindows
nodes.doc: appWindows returns retained AXUIElementRefs for an app's standard, unminimized windows. Each ref is owned by the caller: keep it as a window handle and releaseRef it when the window goes away.

nodes.label: closeWindow
nodes.doc: closeWindow asks the window to close by pressing its close button — the public-API equivalent of clicking the red dot, so apps get to prompt about unsaved changes instead of being killed.

nodes.label: displays
nodes.doc: displays lists active displays as [id, x, y, w, h] entries (CG global coordinates, top-left origin), main display first — the stable ordering the `tile_display` config indexes into.

nodes.label: focusWindowNoRaise
nodes.doc: focusWindowNoRaise moves keyboard focus without restacking — what focus-follows-mouse wants (X11 ffm does not raise on hover either).

nodes.label: focusedWindowRef
nodes.doc: focusedWindowRef returns a retained ref to the system-focused window, or null. Compare against managed handles with sameRef, then releaseRef it.

nodes.label: isMinimized
nodes.doc: isMinimized reads a window's live AXMinimized flag. A minimized window stays alive and listed in the app's AXWindows array — it just drops out of the tiling scan, since appWindows filters it via isStandardWindow below. The recall path uses this to tell a stashed window apart from a visible one.

nodes.label: menuBarHeight
nodes.doc: menuBarHeight is the system menu bar's height in points, so the status bar can match it. Requires AppKit to be loaded (cocoa\uiInit) first.

nodes.label: mouseLocation
nodes.doc: mouseLocation reads the cursor position in global top-left coordinates — the same space as window frames. The underlying CGEventGetLocation returns a CGPoint by value (which upstream's zdef JIT can't carry), so yt_cursor does the CGEventCreate + read in C and writes {x, y} as two f64 into our buffer.

nodes.label: moveWindow
nodes.doc: moveWindow sets only the window's position (AXPosition), leaving its size. Callers park off-workspace windows with state\parkX/parkY: no minimize genie, no Dock churn, and the window keeps its exact layout for when its workspace returns. NB this does NOT move a window off-screen. macOS clamps AXPosition so the title bar stays on the display, so a far-off park target lands as a ~40px nub in the corner (the AX limit corner-parking lives with); a user drag is clamped the same way.

nodes.label: onScreenPids
nodes.doc: onScreenPids lists owner pids of normal (layer-0) on-screen windows, front to back, deduplicated. The window list is the only public way to learn which apps currently have real windows up.

nodes.label: raiseWindow
nodes.doc: raiseWindow brings a window to the front of its stack without touching focus — the stacked layout re-raises on every retile, so it must not fight the user over keyboard focus the way focusWindow would.

nodes.label: setAppHidden
nodes.doc: setAppHidden hides/shows an entire app (the cmd+H equivalent) via the app's AXHidden attribute — instant, no per-window animation. All-or-nothing per app.

nodes.label: setMinimized
nodes.doc: setMinimized parks or restores a window. bubblegum's workspaces are built on this: macOS Spaces APIs are private, but minimizing is plain public AX.

nodes.label: setNativeFullscreen
nodes.doc: setNativeFullscreen drives macOS's own green-button fullscreen (the window gets its own Space) through the public AXFullScreen attribute. While fullscreen the window leaves the tiled display, so the scanner releases it back to the system; on exit it is re-adopted — by design.

nodes.label: setWindowFrame
nodes.doc: setWindowFrame moves then resizes via the AX value API: CGPoint/CGSize structs are written field-by-field into pinned memory and passed by reference, which is the AXValueCreate calling convention.

nodes.label: viewFrameCG
nodes.doc: viewFrameCG returns an NSView's window frame as [x, y, w, h, primaryHeight]: the first four in CG global coords (top-left — the space mouse clicks arrive in), the last the main display's height in points (to convert back to AppKit's bottom-left origin). Empty if the view has no window yet. Used to locate the menu-bar status item so its clicks can be hit-tested.

nodes.label: windowAlive
nodes.doc: windowAlive reports whether the element still backs a real window; parked (minimized) windows are alive, closed ones answer with an AX error.

nodes.label: windowFrame
nodes.doc: windowFrame reads the current [x, y, w, h], or null when the window is gone (its app quit between scans, say).

nodes.label: charCode
nodes.doc: charCode is the byte value of a single-character string (ASCII patterns).

nodes.label: classEnd
nodes.doc: classEnd finds the index of the closing ']' (escapes skipped); -1 if none.

nodes.label: classTok
nodes.doc: classTok expands a class body ("a-z0-9_", leading ^ negates) into a member set; null when empty or a range is inverted.

nodes.label: compileAlt
nodes.doc: compileAlt tokenizes one alternative; null on malformed input. Returned as [any] (the untyped-accumulator idiom); reads re-type via tokAt.

nodes.label: matchStar
nodes.doc: matchStar: greedy, then give back one at a time (classic backtracking).

nodes.label: reCompile
nodes.doc: reCompile compiles src, or returns null when it is malformed (unclosed class, dangling escape or repetition, empty alternative).

nodes.label: reMatch
nodes.doc: reMatch reports whether text contains a match (anchor with ^/$ for exact).

nodes.label: splitAlternatives
nodes.doc: splitAlternatives splits on `|` outside classes and escapes.

nodes.label: barCountHit
nodes.doc: barCountHit reports whether a bar-local x lands on the centered window-count label. That label doubles as the minimized indicator (⊟ app, app); clicking it opens the recall picker. The box mirrors countLabel's frame in barInit (w/2 - 110 - 9, w 220).

nodes.label: barIconAt
nodes.doc: barIconAt maps a bar-local x to a right-edge icon slot: 0 = cheatsheet, 1 = console, 2 = reconcile, or -1 if x is outside the cluster.

nodes.label: barInit
nodes.doc: barInit places the bar along the bottom edge of the tiled display: x/y are the display's AppKit origin (bottom-left), w its width. The look follows the toolkit theme (cocoa\uiTheme): macOS = menu material + dynamic colors + SF Symbol icons + accent pill; i3 = solid dark bar + light text + glyph icons + blue block.

nodes.label: barInitIcons
nodes.doc: barInitIcons drops the three clickable icons into the right-edge cluster, each with a hover tooltip. Their hit zones are barIconAt's. macOS uses tinted SF Symbols; i3 uses monochrome glyph labels.

nodes.label: barOrientHit
nodes.doc: barOrientHit reports whether a bar-local x lands on the orientation glyph — the band just left of the reconcile-pause toggle.

nodes.label: barPlusHit
nodes.doc: barPlusHit reports whether a bar-local x lands on the "+" cell (the slot immediately after the last visible workspace). Returns false when all 9 workspace slots are already shown (no room for a new one).

nodes.label: barSetVisible
nodes.doc: barSetVisible follows the config's `bar on|off` across live reloads.

nodes.label: barToggleHit
nodes.doc: barToggleHit reports whether a bar-local x lands on the reconcile-pause toggle — the rightmost glyph of the right label, which now right-aligns just left of the icon cluster (barInit shifts it by iconClusterW).

nodes.label: barUpdate
nodes.doc: barUpdate redraws the mode tag, the centered window count, the right-aligned status/eval text, and the orientation/auto-reconcile indicators on change only — uiSetText allocates an NSString per call and the bar refreshes every poll tick. The left side (number cells + pills + mode-tag position) is barWorkspaces' job. orient ("", "horiz", "vert", "stack") and paused drive the indicators — SF Symbol icons on macOS, glyphs appended to the right text on i3. Suppressed while a prompt owns the bar.

nodes.label: barWorkspaces
nodes.doc: barWorkspaces paints the left side: workspace cells for the current and every occupied workspace (packed together with no gaps), the accent pill on the active slot, a subtle pill on every other occupied slot, a "+" button after the last cell (when fewer than 9 slots are shown), and the mode tag floated past them. Diffed on an (active, shown, occupancy) signature so the 50ms refresh doesn't re-lay-out a left side that didn't change. Suppressed while a prompt owns the bar.

nodes.label: barWsAt
nodes.doc: barWsAt maps a bar-local x to a workspace index (0-based), or -1 if x is not on any visible workspace cell. cur and counts are used to map display slots to real workspace indices (populated workspaces are packed without gaps).

nodes.label: displaySlotOf
nodes.doc: displaySlotOf maps a workspace index to its packed display position (0-based), or -1 if that workspace is neither current nor occupied.

nodes.label: numX
nodes.doc: numX is where a cell's number label sits — the cell, nudged right by numInset so the centered digit lands on the pill center.

nodes.label: occupiedCell
nodes.doc: occupiedCell reports whether workspace cell i wears the subtle "occupied" pill: it holds windows and isn't the active cell (which wears the accent pill instead).

nodes.label: orientGlyph
nodes.doc: orientGlyph / orientSymbol map the next-split orientation to its i3 glyph and its macOS SF Symbol. "" means no orientation indicator (fewer than two tiled windows).

nodes.label: promptSetText
nodes.doc: promptSetText updates only the prompt's message, not the wash/layout, so the WM can tick a live countdown into the prompt without redoing the whole bar takeover.

nodes.label: promptShow
nodes.doc: promptShow takes the bar over with `text` and washes the whole bar in the accent color so it reads as active, not a passive status. The icons stay put; the workspace/right text is replaced. promptHide reverts on the next barUpdate.

nodes.label: trayButton
nodes.doc: trayButton returns the menu-bar item's button (an NSView), or null before trayInit — the WM passes it to windows\viewFrameCG to learn where the tray icon sits so it can hit-test clicks landing on it.

nodes.label: trayInit
nodes.doc: trayInit drops bubblegum into the system status bar; trayUpdate keeps it honest ("⊞ 3" = tiling, workspace 3). macOS theme uses an SF Symbol; i3 uses a glyph. The text passed to trayUpdate is the live part (workspace number or bind mode).

nodes.label: wsAtSlot
nodes.doc: wsAtSlot returns the workspace index at display slot `slot`, or -1 if out of range.

nodes.label: wsShown
nodes.doc: wsShown counts the workspace cells the bar renders: the current workspace plus every occupied workspace, packed together with no gaps between them.

nodes.label: toastShow
nodes.doc: toastShow centers the panel horizontally in [ax, ax + aw] and hangs it just below ayTop (the AppKit top edge of the tiling area), where macOS notifications live. The WM hides it later via toastHide once the deadline it recorded has passed.

nodes.label: menuHeight
nodes.doc: menuHeight is the panel height for n rows — the WM needs it to place the panel (it drops down from the tray icon) and to size the click target. With items present (n >= 2) it reserves headerGap below the header for the divider + space.

nodes.label: rowAt
nodes.doc: rowAt maps a y measured DOWN from the menu's top edge to a row index, or -1 if it lands in the padding, the header gap, or past the last shown row. The header (row 0) sits in the top slot; the items below are offset by headerGap.

nodes.label: setRowText
nodes.doc: setRowText updates a single row's text without rebuilding the whole menu: the WM ticks the pause countdown on the Resume row this way.

nodes.label: show
nodes.doc: show draws the menu with its bottom-left at AppKit (x, y) and the given rows (header first). `paused` colors the status dot: green running, amber paused. Repositions an already-built panel and parks any unused labels offscreen.

nodes.label: artifactPath
nodes.doc: artifactPath mirrors core/paths\configDir() exactly (same process, same env, so the same path), with the artifact file the running WM reads back. Kept here rather than importing core/paths so the public package carries no dependency on WM internals.

nodes.label: bind
nodes.doc: bind appends a keybinding. The chord is validated and resolved on the WM side (config\parseChord lives there), so a bad chord is reported once, where it lands.

nodes.label: clearBinds
nodes.doc: clearBinds drops the default keymap so the config can build one from scratch.

nodes.label: configure
nodes.doc: configure applies a typed Config. It resolves the Config to the live Runtime's flat scalar form (enums to their string names, the cheat chord to flags/keycode) by reusing config\applyConfig, then snapshots those scalars into the op so the WM assigns them directly without re-deriving anything.

nodes.label: flush
nodes.doc: flush rewrites the artifact with the ops recorded so far. Called after every verb so a config that throws partway still leaves the WM a faithful record of what ran before the error (the WM only applies it on a clean run).

nodes.label: rule
nodes.doc: rule appends a window rule (i3 for_window). Parsed on the WM side (config\parseRule).

nodes.label: square
nodes.doc: square is handed to C as a function pointer via ffi.callback below.

nodes.label: ci
nodes.doc: The anchor `magus affected ci` keys off; fans out lint/build/test after format.

nodes.label: generate
nodes.doc: Regenerates MAGUS.md and fails on drift. Use the rw charm to keep the result. Drift = regeneration dirtied a file that was clean, via vcs.isDirty (not a raw git/porcelain shell-out), so a locally-regenerated MAGUS.md passes whether or not it is committed; only a regeneration that changes a clean file fails.

nodes.label: md_generate
nodes.doc: Renders MAGUS.md (target catalog plus graph) from this magusfile.

nodes.label: pgo_generate
nodes.doc: Regenerates default.pgo, the Buzz VM's PGO profile. Re-run after material changes to the interpreter hot path. The canonical profile lives here; cmd/magus/default.pgo symlinks to it so `go build ./cmd/magus` (-pgo=auto) applies it.

nodes.label: contains
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `container` holds `item`: a substring of a str, an element (by / deep equality) of a list, or a key of a map. / @param container The str, list, or map to search / @param item The substring, element, or key to find / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: didThrow
nodes.doc: / Returns true when calling `fn` raises. The `catch` default turns a raised / error into `false` (the call did not complete), which we invert. / @param fn The zero-argument function to probe / @return Whether fn raised

nodes.label: doesNotThrow
nodes.doc: / Asserts that calling `fn` does NOT raise. / @param fn The zero-argument function expected to complete / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: equal
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `got` is structurally (deeply) equal to `want`: same scalars / (ints and doubles compare across type), same list elements in order, same map / key/value pairs regardless of order, recursing to any depth. Use this instead / of `==`, which compares identity for maps, lists, and objects. / @param got The value under test / @param want The expected value / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: fail
nodes.doc: / Fails the current test unconditionally. / @param message Why the test failed

nodes.label: greater
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `a` is greater than `b`. Both must be two numbers or two strings. / @param a The value expected to be larger / @param b The value expected to be smaller / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: invoke
nodes.doc: / Calls `fn` and returns true once it completes; propagates any error it raises. / @param fn The zero-argument function to call / @return true when fn returns normally

nodes.label: isEmpty
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `container` is empty (zero-length str, list, or map). / @param container The str, list, or map to measure / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: isFalse
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `got` is false. / @param got The value under test / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: isNull
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `got` is null. / @param got The value under test / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: isTrue
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `got` is true. / @param got The value under test / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: len
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `container` has exactly `want` elements (str codepoints, list / items, or map keys). / @param container The str, list, or map to measure / @param want The expected length / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: less
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `a` is less than `b`. Both must be two numbers or two strings. / @param a The value expected to be smaller / @param b The value expected to be larger / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: notEqual
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `got` is NOT structurally equal to `want`. / @param got The value under test / @param want The value it should differ from / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: notNull
nodes.doc: / Asserts that `got` is not null. / @param got The value under test / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: skip
nodes.doc: / Skips the current test: stops the block and marks it skipped (not failed), the / way Go's t.Skip does. Use it for a case that cannot run in the current / environment (platform, missing fixture). The runner reports it separately. / @param message Why the test was skipped

nodes.label: tail
nodes.doc: / Renders the message as " (message)", or "" when it is empty. / @param message The assertion message / @return The bracketed suffix, or ""

nodes.label: throws
nodes.doc: / Asserts that calling `fn` raises. Pair it with a `test` block to check that a / function rejects bad input. / @param fn The zero-argument function expected to throw / @param message The assertion message

nodes.label: new
nodes.doc: / Creates a Suite with optional lifecycle hooks (pass null to skip one). Buzz has / no static methods, so a Suite is built through this free function rather than a / constructor. / @param setupAll Hook run once before the first test / @param setupEach Hook run before each test / @param teardownAll Hook run once after the last test / @param teardownEach Hook run after each test / @return A new Suite

nodes.label: noop
nodes.doc: / A hook that does nothing, substituted for a null lifecycle hook.

nodes.label: tail
nodes.doc: / Renders the message as " (message)", or "" when empty. / @param message The assertion message / @return The bracketed suffix, or ""

nodes.label: paint
nodes.doc: paint wraps text in an ANSI SGR color, or returns it untouched when the output is not a color terminal (NO_COLOR set, per no-color.org, or Windows console).

nodes.label: after
nodes.doc: after inserts vals immediately after the first argv element equal to anchor.

nodes.label: append
nodes.doc: append adds vals to the end of the argv.

nodes.label: before
nodes.doc: before inserts vals immediately before the first argv element equal to anchor.

nodes.label: drop
nodes.doc: drop removes the first argv element equal to anchor. Named drop, not remove, to match the host charm.drop (where "remove" collides with the map method) so the two modules expose one name.

nodes.label: prepend
nodes.doc: prepend inserts vals at the front of the argv, in order.

nodes.label: set
nodes.doc: set replaces the first argv element equal to anchor with val.

nodes.label: badge_color
nodes.doc: Maps a coverage percentage to a go-badge color name.

nodes.label: bindings_generate
